questions.question_text: What must every real estate advertisement in Alaska include?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name as registered with AREC
distractors: The salesperson's personal license number
The AREC logo and commission phone number
The listing price and property address
questions.explanation: All real estate advertising in Alaska must include the brokerage name as registered with the Commission. This applies to all advertising media — internet, social media, print, signs, business cards, and recruitment materials.

questions.question_text: A real estate team in Alaska creates a social media page advertising their services. What must the page include?
questions.correct_answer: The brokerage name in addition to the team name
distractors: Only the team name is sufficient
The team leader's license number
The brokerage name only if the team has more than 3 members
questions.explanation: Team advertising must still include the brokerage name. Even though the team has its own identity, consumers need to know which brokerage supervises the team's activities. This applies to all advertising including social media.

questions.question_text: A salesperson posts a property listing on their personal website without including the brokerage name. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, the brokerage name must appear on all advertising
distractors: Yes, personal websites are exempt from the requirement
Yes, if the salesperson's license number is included
No, but only if the website generates paid leads
questions.explanation: All advertising, including internet and personal websites, must include the brokerage name as registered with AREC. There is no exemption for personal websites — the brokerage identification requirement applies to all advertising media.

questions.question_text: An Alaska salesperson wants to place a classified ad to sell a listing. Which advertising format is an example of a prohibited blind ad?
questions.correct_answer: An ad with only the salesperson's phone number
distractors: An ad listing the brokerage and property address
An ad with the brokerage name and a team name
An ad showing the property photo and brokerage logo
questions.explanation: A blind ad is one that does not identify the brokerage. An ad with only the salesperson's phone number and no brokerage name is a blind ad and is prohibited in Alaska. Consumers must be able to identify the supervising brokerage.

questions.question_text: A salesperson creates a business card that features only their name, phone number, and the team name. Is this compliant with Alaska advertising rules?
questions.correct_answer: No, the card must include the registered brokerage name
distractors: Yes, business cards are exempt from advertising rules
Yes, if the team is a registered LLC
No, but only if the card is used for solicitation
questions.explanation: Business cards are considered advertising under Alaska rules and must include the brokerage name as registered with AREC. Omitting the brokerage name — even if a team name is included — violates the broker identification requirement.

questions.question_text: A salesperson works from their home office. May they advertise the home address as a brokerage office?
questions.correct_answer: No, they cannot advertise it as an actual brokerage office
distractors: Yes, if they register it with AREC
Yes, if the broker approves in writing
No, but they may advertise it as a branch office
questions.explanation: Alaska specifically prohibits a salesperson or associate broker from advertising the address of a home office as if it were an actual brokerage office. This prevents consumers from being misled about the nature and location of the business.

questions.question_text: A licensee wants to advertise a property for sale. What permission is required before the ad can run?
distractors: AREC's written approval
The local MLS board's authorization
The broker's verbal approval only
questions.explanation: A licensee cannot advertise a property for sale, lease, or rent without the owner's written permission. This protects property owners from unauthorized marketing of their property and ensures the licensee has a legitimate relationship with the seller.

questions.question_text: A listing agent exaggerates a property's square footage in an online ad to attract more buyers. This is an example of what?
distractors: Acceptable marketing strategy
A minor disclosure issue requiring correction
A permissible estimate pending appraisal
questions.explanation: Advertising in a manner that is misleading or deceptive is prohibited in Alaska. Exaggerating square footage could mislead buyers and constitutes a violation. All advertising must be truthful and not create false impressions about the property.

questions.question_text: A salesperson places a 'For Sale' sign on a property without the owner's knowledge. What rule does this violate?
questions.correct_answer: The requirement for owner's written permission to advertise
distractors: The sign ordinance of the local municipality
The broker identification requirement only
The team advertising disclosure requirement
questions.explanation: Placing a sign is a form of advertising, and advertising a property requires the owner's written permission. Placing a sign without the owner's knowledge violates this requirement and could also be considered unauthorized marketing.

questions.question_text: An Alaska brokerage runs a recruitment ad seeking new agents. Must this ad include the brokerage name?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, recruitment materials must identify the brokerage
distractors: No, recruitment ads are exempt from the rule
Only if the ad appears in print media
Only if the ad mentions specific commission splits
questions.explanation: The brokerage identification requirement applies to all advertising, including recruitment materials. Even when soliciting agents rather than clients, the ad must include the brokerage name as registered with AREC.
